topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a grating soundtrack to my internal turmoil. I stared at the spreadsheet, the numbers swimming before my eyes. It was a simple comparison, yet the disparity between my monthly salary and that of Mark, the junior analyst, felt like a physical weight pressing down on my chest. Every click of the mouse sounded amplified in the quiet cubicle. I forced myself to breathe slowly, counting to five in, six out, trying to anchor myself to the reality of the quarterly reports instead of the stark difference in our paychecks. When I finally looked up, Mark was across the aisle, smiling easily as he leaned back in his chair, a casualness that felt like a deliberate challenge. My jaw tightened, and I picked up my pen with unnecessary force, the plastic feeling slick in my clammy hand.

[story 2]

My palms were slick against the keyboard. I kept glancing over my shoulder, half-expecting someone to catch me staring at the screen. The figures were undeniable, stark evidence laid out in bold print. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat making the action difficult. It wasn't just the money; it was the implication—the unfairness of the structure. I started meticulously re-reading the performance metrics, searching for any justification, any loophole, anything to rationalize the gap. A slight tremor ran through my fingers as I highlighted a specific project cost, my gaze fixed on the screen, refusing to meet anyone's eyes. The air in the room seemed to thicken, making every small movement feel exaggerated and scrutinized.

[story 3]

I retreated to the breakroom, needing space away from the open-plan chaos. Leaning against the counter, I traced patterns on the condensation of my water glass. The silence of the room was deceptive; it was filled with the echo of my own rapid heartbeat. The realization had settled in my gut like cold stone. I could feel a flush creeping up my neck, hot and unwelcome. When Sarah walked in, her cheerful greeting seemed jarringly loud. I managed a tight, strained smile, nodding vaguely, my focus entirely on keeping my expression neutral, a carefully constructed mask over the churning uncertainty inside me. I just wanted the moment to pass, to dissolve into the mundane routine of making coffee.
